behaviors.description: Required patient fields validated before downstream processing — missing fields produce clear error listing all missing items.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is purely within one validation function — does it check all required fields and aggregate missing ones into a single error? No module boundary is crossed. REAL-INFRA: No OS, network, or DB. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Integration would require wiring to downstream modules, but the failure (wrong error message, partial missing-field list) manifests entirely within the validation function itself.
behaviors.test_description: Parametrize with inputs missing one, two, or all three required fields (name, DOB, insurance ID). Assert that the error is raised before any downstream call and that the error payload names every missing field. Also test the all-fields-present case returns no error.

behaviors.description: Date of birth validation rejects future dates, dates more than 150 years ago, and non-date strings.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: All three rejection conditions are algorithmic comparisons inside a single parsing-and-validation function — no module handoff needed. REAL-INFRA: No external I/O; today's date can be injected or mocked at the unit level. LLM-DEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Nothing in the behavior description requires downstream modules to interact for the failure to appear.
behaviors.test_description: Parametrize over: (a) future date (today+1 day), (b) date exactly 150 years ago (boundary valid) and 150 years + 1 day ago (boundary invalid), (c) non-date strings like 'abc' and '13/32/2020'. Assert each invalid input raises a typed validation error with a meaningful message; assert a valid DOB passes cleanly.

behaviors.description: Insurance eligibility check sends correct patient ID and plan code to payer API and correctly maps 'eligible', 'not eligible', and 'pending' responses.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure lives at the boundary between the eligibility service module and the HTTP client — wrong field serialization or wrong response-to-status mapping only appears when these two modules are wired. REAL-INFRA: A stub HTTP server (or intercepting HTTP client) can capture the outbound request payload and return each of the three response variants — real network is not needed to expose the failure. LLM-DEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: I considered System (real payer API), but the exact failure modes — wrong field names in the request body, wrong status mapping — are fully observable with an in-process stub that records requests and returns controlled responses.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the eligibility module to a stub HTTP handler. For each of the three payer API response shapes (eligible, not eligible, pending), assert (a) the outbound request contains exactly the patient ID and plan code from the intake record, and (b) the returned eligibility status matches the expected mapping. Cover all three variants in separate parametrized cases.

behaviors.description: When the payer API is unreachable, the system marks eligibility as 'pending' and continues the intake rather than blocking.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure requires the eligibility module and the intake orchestrator to be wired — the orchestrator must catch the propagated exception and set state correctly; neither module alone exhibits the bug. REAL-INFRA: A stub HTTP client that raises a connection error (ConnectionError, timeout) triggers the exact code path; real network unreachability is not needed. LLM-DEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: I considered System (real downed network), but the fault path is an exception being raised and caught — an in-process stub raising the exception exercises the identical code branch.
behaviors.test_description: Configure a stub HTTP client that raises a connection-timeout exception on the payer API call. Run the intake flow with a valid patient record. Assert that (a) the eligibility field is set to 'pending', (b) the intake flow does not raise or halt, and (c) subsequent steps (triage, scheduling) still execute.

behaviors.description: AI symptom triage correctly assigns high urgency to symptom combinations that indicate emergency conditions (e.g., chest pain + shortness of breath).
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: The correctness claim is about the AI medical reasoning engine's clinical judgment — whether it assigns 'high urgency' given emergency symptom combinations. A stub that returns a fixed urgency level will always pass regardless of the real model's capability. MOCK-HIDE: Any deterministic mock hides the exact failure the behavior describes: the model misclassifying an emergency. REJECTION-GATE: I considered Integration (test the routing/request structure), but the behavior text is explicitly about correct urgency assignment, not about request plumbing — that is model output content, not infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a real triage request containing a known emergency symptom combination (chest pain + shortness of breath) to the actual AI medical reasoning engine. Assert the returned urgency level is 'high'. Optionally include a non-emergency combination (mild headache + fatigue) and assert it does not return 'high', providing a contrast case.

behaviors.description: Symptom triage produces a natural language explanation of its urgency assessment that a clinician can review, not just a numeric score.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: 'A natural language explanation that a clinician can review' is a quality requirement on AI-generated content. A stub returning 'High urgency detected.' satisfies any structural format check while hiding the real failure: the model producing an empty string, a numeric label, or a clinically meaningless output. MOCK-HIDE: Deterministic stubs always pass the format check; only a real model call reveals whether the explanation is substantive. REJECTION-GATE: I considered Integration (verify the system requests a text field and passes it through), but the behavior text specifies what is produced — content that a clinician can review — not merely that a text field is present in the response schema.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real AI triage engine with a representative symptom set. Assert the response contains a string explanation field that (a) is non-empty, (b) contains no bare numeric tokens as the entire content, and (c) references at least one of the submitted symptoms, confirming the explanation is grounded rather than generic.

behaviors.description: Appointment is scheduled in the first available slot matching the assigned urgency level — high urgency same-day, medium urgency within 48 hours.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the scheduling module's slot-selection logic interacting with calendar state — providing multiple available slots and verifying the first correct one is chosen requires the scheduler and calendar store to be wired. REAL-INFRA: A local test database or in-memory calendar store reproduces the slot-selection failure; no real OS or network needed. LLM-DEP: Urgency level is an input parameter here — the behavior tests scheduling logic given a known urgency, so the AI triage output is not a variable. REJECTION-GATE: Unit is too low because slot selection depends on querying stored calendar state, not just one function's algorithm.
behaviors.test_description: Seed a test calendar database with a mix of slots: same-day slots, next-day slots, and 3-day-out slots. Invoke the scheduler with urgency='high' and assert the selected slot is same-day. Invoke with urgency='medium' and assert the slot is within 48 hours. Assert in both cases that the very first matching slot (not a later one) is chosen.

behaviors.description: Scheduling step does not double-book a time slot even when two patients are being processed concurrently.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is a race condition that only surfaces when two scheduling operations interact through shared database state — neither module in isolation shows the bug. REAL-INFRA: Requires real concurrent execution (threads or async tasks) against a database that enforces transactional isolation; an in-process test DB (e.g., local PostgreSQL or SQLite with WAL mode) can reproduce the failure. A purely in-memory mock with no real locking semantics would hide the race. REJECTION-GATE: I considered System (separate processes), but threading against a local test DB is sufficient to expose a missing DB-level lock or unique constraint — no real OS process isolation or network is required.
behaviors.test_description: With a local test database, launch two concurrent scheduling requests (threads or async tasks) targeting the same single available time slot. After both complete, query the slot and assert it is booked exactly once. The test must confirm one booking succeeded and the other received a conflict/retry response, not that both silently succeeded.

behaviors.description: Confirmation message includes patient name, appointment time, provider name, and preparation instructions appropriate to the visit type.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The confirmation assembles data from at least two sources (patient record, scheduling result) and applies visit-type logic to select preparation instructions — the failure (missing field, wrong instructions for visit type) appears at the boundary where these sources are combined, not inside any single function. REAL-INFRA: No OS or network; a local data store or in-memory fixture provides the source records. LLM-DEP: Preparation instructions are described as visit-type-appropriate (rule/template-based), not AI-generated. REJECTION-GATE: I considered Unit, but 'appropriate to the visit type' implies a lookup or branching over visit-type data that couples the confirmation module to a content store.
behaviors.test_description: For each of at least two distinct visit types, build a composite test record (patient + scheduling result) and invoke the confirmation generator. Assert the output contains the exact patient name, appointment time formatted correctly, provider name, and visit-type-specific preparation instructions. Assert no placeholder or empty fields appear.

behaviors.description: Full intake pipeline — validate, check insurance, triage symptoms, schedule, confirm — completes for a real patient submission without manual intervention.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: The behavior explicitly names five sequential steps, one of which (symptom triage) requires a real AI medical reasoning engine call. The end-to-end failure can only appear when all steps are connected — e.g., triage output drives scheduling urgency, scheduling result drives confirmation content. LLM-DEP: The triage step uses a real AI engine; its output affects downstream steps. REAL-INFRA: Insurance check makes a real or realistic HTTP call; scheduling writes to a shared DB. No single lower level captures cross-step data-flow failures. REJECTION-GATE: Agentic covers one AI call but not the full sequential pipeline where each step's output is the next step's input — that is the definition of Workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a complete patient intake record (all required fields, valid insurance ID, a known symptom set) through the full pipeline against real or realistic services (live AI triage engine, stub payer API returning 'eligible', local test calendar DB). Assert that each stage completes without error, the final confirmation message contains correct patient and appointment data, and no stage required manual intervention. Capture and assert the intermediate outputs (eligibility status, urgency level, scheduled slot) to confirm correct data propagation across steps.
